params.text:
PEG, PEG-J and NG tubes

Not every feeding tube is the same, and it helps to recognise the differences. A PEG ends in the stomach. A PEG-J has an extra portion that passes on from the stomach into the small bowel (the jejunum), used for people who cannot tolerate feed in the stomach. A nasogastric (NG) tube is passed through the nose down into the stomach and is usually for shorter-term feeding.

  • PEG: feeds into the stomach through the abdomen.
  • PEG-J: feeds beyond the stomach into the small bowel.
  • NG tube: passes through the nose, usually short term.

params.alt: Illustration for Types and your role

params.text:
Your role and your limits

Your role in enteral feeding is important but clearly bounded. You may be delegated everyday tasks such as setting up a prescribed feed, giving water flushes, supporting stoma site care and helping the person stay comfortable — but only after task-specific training, assessment of your competence, and written delegation by a registered nurse or dietitian. You must never insert, remove or replace a tube, change what has been prescribed, or start giving feed into a tube you are unsure about.

An NG tube in particular must have its position confirmed by a trained person before every feed, because it can move; if you have not been trained and assessed to do this, you must not use it. Whatever the type of tube, the golden rule is the same: if anything is different from usual, or you are not certain the tube is safe to use, stop and escalate to the nurse or dietitian rather than carry on.

params.question: A person's PEG tube looks different from how the care plan describes it, and you are not sure it is safe to use. What should you do?

answers.text: Stop, do not start the feed, and escalate to the registered nurse or dietitian.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Never use a tube you are unsure about — stop and escalate so a professional can check it.

answers.text: Start the feed anyway, as the person is due their nutrition.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No — never start feed through a tube you are unsure about; the risk to the person is too high.

answers.text: Try to push the tube back into position yourself.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No — adjusting or repositioning a tube is not your role; escalate to a nurse or dietitian.

answers.text: Wait until the end of your shift and mention it to the next carer.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No — a possible problem with a feeding tube needs to be escalated promptly, not left.
